compiled_state.title: Intent-Aware Bug Classification Gates
compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart
compiled_state.description:
## Context
After Epic A (2df5-72cb) restructures debug-everything to invoke fix-bug at the orchestrator level, fix-bug still auto-approves fixes that alter architecture, reverse intentional design decisions, or weaken test coverage — causing silent drift from user intent. Practitioners must manually review each bug to assess whether autonomous fixing is safe. This epic adds a three-layer classification gate that automates that assessment: confirming intent from project history, detecting feature requests, flagging test regressions, and routing proportionally to risk.

## Success Criteria
1. Before fix-bug begins investigation, a timeboxed intent search (configurable via debug.intent_search_budget in dso-config.conf, default 20 tool calls) classifies the bug as intent-aligned, intent-contradicting, or ambiguous. Intent-aligned bugs proceed without user dialog. Intent-contradicting bugs are auto-closed with a ticket comment citing the evidence source (ticket ID, commit hash, or doc path). Ambiguous bugs fall through to the feature-vs-bug language check.
2. Bugs whose ticket title or description matches feature-request language patterns ("doesn't support/accept/handle X", "missing X capability") are flagged as potential feature requests before investigation begins — verified by a test suite of 10+ ticket descriptions with known correct classifications
3. A fix that removes a test assertion, replaces a specific expected value with a broader matcher (e.g., assertEqual(x, 42) to assertIsNotNone(x)), or reduces assertion count in a test file is flagged for user dialog before implementation. A fix that replaces one specific expected value with a different specific expected value (e.g., assertEqual(x, 42) to assertEqual(x, 57)) proceeds without interruption. When Gate 1a has confirmed the behavior being unblocked is documented as expected, a false-positive correction in a gate is treated as "correcting to match intent" and does not fire Gate 2c.
4. A fix that introduces a runtime or dev dependency not already in the project, or introduces an architectural pattern not already used in the codebase, is flagged for user dialog. Reusing an existing codebase pattern (e.g., adding a sub-agent dispatch where other dispatches already exist) does not trigger this gate.
5. Blast radius is estimated using two inputs: (a) file location conventions detected during /dso:onboarding (package manifests, CI/CD config, schema/migration files, entry points), and (b) fan-in count from static import analysis (via ast-grep when available, falling back to grep-based import counting when ast-grep is not installed). The result is reported as a plain-language annotation in escalation dialog messages (e.g., "Note: this file is imported by 12 other modules"). Blast radius never adds a primary escalation signal — it only enriches dialog text when another gate has already triggered. SC3 operates within fix-bug on proposed changes before commit; Centrality-Aware Test Gate operates at commit time on staged files — no shared enforcement logic.
6. When zero primary gates fire, the fix proceeds autonomously. When exactly one primary gate fires, fix-bug prompts a 1-2 question inline dialog. When two or more primary gates fire, the bug is escalated to /dso:brainstorm for epic treatment. The complexity evaluator returning COMPLEX always escalates to epic regardless of other signals.

## Dependencies
- Epic A: Debug-Everything Restructure (2df5-72cb) — blocking. Classification gates run inside fix-bug at the orchestrator level, which Epic A enables. Without Epic A, fix-bug runs as a sub-agent and cannot dispatch the intent search sub-agent.
- ast-grep integration epic — enhancing, not blocking. Fan-in analysis falls back to grep-based import counting when ast-grep is not available. The blast radius gate functions without ast-grep but with reduced accuracy.

## Approach
Three-layer intent classification gate integrated into fix-bug's existing workflow. Layer 1 (pre-investigation, in orchestrator): Gate 1a timeboxed intent search + Gate 1b feature-vs-bug language check. Layer 2 (post-investigation, in fix-bug after fix is proposed): Gate 2a reversal check against git history, Gate 2b blast radius (modifier only), Gate 2c test regression analysis against fix proposal, Gate 2d new dependency/architecture check. Layer 3 (post-fix-proposal): Gate 3a complexity evaluator. Gate 1a's three outcomes (intent-aligned, intent-contradicting, ambiguous) are terminal and immutable once set — Gate 2c reads this as a boolean flag. Post-delivery audit against 10 historical bugs with known correct routing serves as acceptance test.

## Scenario Analysis
- Partial evidence ambiguity (high): contradictory partial evidence returns "no-evidence" (fail toward dialog)
- Escalation math miscount (high): 1a result is immutable boolean flag, no ambiguous state
- Revert-of-revert false positive (medium): Gate 2a recognizes revert-of-revert patterns in commit messages

### Planning Intelligence Log
- Web research (Step 2.6): not triggered
- Scenario analysis (Step 2.75): triggered
  - Scenarios surviving blue team filter: 10
- Practitioner-requested additional cycles: none

comments.body: Adversarial review: 8 findings, 7 accepted. Full exchange: /tmp/workflow-plugin-886c4e92a94d7e0e/adversarial-review-a5f2-1811.json
comments.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body: Epic complete: all 8 stories closed, all 6 success criteria verified by completion verifier. 107 tests pass across 10 test files. All 6 gate scripts smoke-test clean.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart
